content:
# Disagreements — Genesis 46 (GEN_046)

## 1. Yashit Yado Al-Einekha — "Put His Hand on Your Eyes" vs. "Close Your Eyes" (v. 4)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Joseph will put his hand on your eyes" | Literal: yashit yado al-einekha |
| LOGOS | "Joseph's hand shall close your eyes" | Interprets the idiom — "close" for "put hand on" |
| PNEUMA | "Joseph — his own hands will close your eyes" | Dynamic; uses plural "hands" (follows LXX, not MT) |
| **SOPHIA** | **"Joseph will put his hand on your eyes" — EMET upheld** | The Hebrew yashit yado al-einekha literally means "will put/place his hand upon your eyes" — the ancient death-closing gesture. EMET preserves both the singular "hand" (yado, not yadav/hands) and the concrete image of placing rather than "closing." LOGOS's "close" interprets the effect rather than rendering the action. PNEUMA follows the LXX's plural "hands" (χεῖρας) rather than the MT's singular yad — a departure from our MT-primary convention. The literal idiom is tender and clear; it does not need interpretation. |

## 2. Anokhi + Gam-Aloh — Double Emphasis in the Divine Promise (v. 4)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "I will also surely bring you up" | Captures infinitive absolute (gam-aloh) but drops second anokhi |
| LOGOS | "I myself will also bring you up again" | Captures anokhi but drops infinitive absolute emphasis |
| PNEUMA | "I myself will surely bring you back up again" | Captures both anokhi and emphasis |
| **SOPHIA** | **"I myself will surely bring you up again" — Blend** | The Hebrew ve'anokhi a'alkha gam-aloh contains two emphatic elements: the pronoun anokhi ("I myself") and the infinitive absolute aloh ("surely"). EMET captures "surely" but omits the second "I myself." LOGOS captures "I myself" but omits "surely." Both elements are in the Hebrew; both should be in the English. SOPHIA renders: "I myself will surely bring you up again." |

## 3. Nephesh in Genealogical Counting — "Souls" vs. "Persons" (vv. 15, 18, 22, 25, 26, 27)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "souls" throughout | nephesh = soul, whole living being |
| LOGOS | "persons" throughout | nephesh as counting unit = person |
| PNEUMA | "persons" throughout | Same as LOGOS |
| **SOPHIA** | **"souls" — EMET upheld** | The Hebrew kol-nephesh ("all the souls") uses nephesh — the word for the whole animated self — as the counting unit throughout this register. "Seventy souls" is the canonical expression for Israel entering Egypt (Deut 10:22; Acts 7:14 uses psychas/souls). Rendering nephesh as "persons" is accurate but loses the Hebrew's way of counting people as whole living beings. The theological weight of the seventy-soul number is better served by preserving "souls." |

## 4. Yotse'ei Yerekho — "Out of His Loins" vs. "From His Own Body" vs. "His Own Flesh and Blood" (v. 26)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "those who came out of his loins" | yerekh = thigh/loin — literal |
| LOGOS | "those who came from his own body" | Interprets yerekh as "body" |
| PNEUMA | "those who were his own flesh and blood" | Paraphrases entirely |
| **SOPHIA** | **"those who came out of his loins" — EMET upheld** | Hebrew yerekh means "thigh, loin" — a standard biblical euphemism for male generative origin (cf. Gen 24:2, 47:29). "Loins" preserves the Hebrew idiom and its physical concreteness. "Body" is vague; "flesh and blood" is a complete paraphrase that loses the specific anatomical reference. |

## 5. Lehorot Lefanav — "To Direct" vs. "To Show the Way" vs. "To Prepare the Way" (v. 28)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "to direct before him" | lehorot = hiphil of yarah, "to direct, instruct" |
| LOGOS | "to show the way before him" | Same root; more natural English |
| PNEUMA | "to prepare the way before him" | Interpretive — yarah ≠ "prepare" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"to show the way before him" — LOGOS upheld** | The hiphil of yarah means "to direct, instruct, point out the way." EMET's "to direct" is accurate but slightly stiff. LOGOS's "to show the way" preserves the directional sense of the verb while being more natural in English. PNEUMA's "to prepare the way" introduces a different concept (preparation vs. direction/instruction). The Torah connection (yarah → Torah) is noted but does not require a different rendering. |

## 6. Od — "Again and Again" vs. "A Good While" vs. "A Long Time" (v. 29)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "wept upon his neck again and again" | od as repeated action |
| LOGOS | "wept on his neck a good while" | od as duration (KJV tradition) |
| PNEUMA | "wept on his neck — a long time" | od as duration (modern paraphrase) |
| **SOPHIA** | **"a good while" — LOGOS upheld** | The adverb od normally means "still, yet, again." Here it modifies weeping to indicate sustained duration. EMET's "again and again" implies discrete repeated bursts; the context describes one continuous, extended weeping. LOGOS's "a good while" follows the Tyndale-KJV tradition and captures the sustained quality of Joseph's embrace without over-specifying. PNEUMA's "a long time" is a simpler paraphrase. "A good while" balances faithfulness and English idiom. |

## 7. Amutah Ha-Pa'am — "Now Let Me Die" vs. "Now I Can Die" (v. 30)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Now let me die" | Cohortative amutah = "let me die" |
| LOGOS | "Now let me die" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "Now I can die" | Changes cohortative to potential |
| **SOPHIA** | **"Now let me die" — EMET/LOGOS upheld (2-1)** | The Hebrew amutah is a cohortative — a first-person volitional form expressing desire or willingness: "let me die." PNEUMA's "I can die" changes the grammatical mood from volitional to potential. Jacob is not merely stating ability but expressing the fulfillment of his deepest longing — death is now acceptable because reunion has occurred. The cohortative must be preserved: "let me die." |

---

*Genesis 46: 7 disagreements logged. SOPHIA ruled with EMET alone on 3 items (v. 4 hand-on-eyes, nephesh/souls, loins), with LOGOS alone on 2 items (v. 28 show-the-way, v. 29 a-good-while), with EMET/LOGOS on 1 item (v. 30 let-me-die), and with a blend on 1 item (v. 4 double emphasis). The genealogy (vv. 8-27) showed near-total agreement across all three translators, with names and counts matching precisely. The major theological moments — the final patriarchal theophany (vv. 2-4), "Here I am" (v. 2), the seventy-soul count (v. 27), the to'evah clause (v. 34) — were rendered with remarkable consistency. Disagreements centered on preserving Hebrew idioms (hand-on-eyes, loins, od) and grammatical moods (cohortative).*
